<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136723510 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00604: Missing data in diagrams, matrices, tables, and relation maps

- page_id: `136723510`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136723510/Missing+data+in+diagrams+matrices+tables+and+relation+maps
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:31:19.030+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > Migration User Guides > UPDM projects migration manual > UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.1 > Mapping issues and solutions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Since UPDM 2 profile [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Removed elements' space='UAF12P2024x'], the Element Type of matrices, tables, and relation maps becomes empty after the migration. The same goes for the customized elements derived from the removed UPDM2 elements. If the UPDM2 [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Replaced and renamed elements' space='UAF12P2024x'] to the UAF element, the UAF element is set as an Element Type (criteria).

#### NOTE: Exception

Exception

If Operational Event Trace, Resource Event Trace, Service Interaction was set as an Element Type criteria, after the migration an Interaction replaces those elements.

After the migration from UPDM 2 to UAF, some tags are no longer supported. During the migration process, all the information from those tags is moved to the**To Do**property of the tag owner. To find this property, open the appropriate element's Specification window. You can display the tag information in the note, anchored to the element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Removed tag added to ToDo property

Condition::conditionKind tag was removed, so after the migration it will be added into ToDo property in the Specification window of the Condition element.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span>Since UPDM 2 profile <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Removed elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[elements were removed]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, the Element Type of matrices, tables, and relation maps becomes empty after the migration. The same goes for the customized elements derived from the removed UPDM2 elements. If the UPDM2 <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Replaced and renamed elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[element is migrated]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to the UAF element, the UAF element is set as an Element Type (criteria).</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4f830871-f7d3-43f9-a110-feeded1958ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Exception</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>If Operational Event Trace, <span>Resource Event Trace, <span>Service Interaction was set as an Element Type criteria, after the migration an Interaction replaces those elements.</span></span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">After the migration from UPDM 2 to UAF, some tags are no longer supported. During the migration process, all the information from those tags is moved to the </span><strong>To Do</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> property of the tag owner. To find this property, open the appropriate element's Specification window. You can display the tag information in the note, anchored to the element.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="migration_tagged_values.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Missing data in diagrams, matrices, tables, and relation maps" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h3>Removed tag added to ToDo property</h3><p>Condition::conditionKind tag was removed, so after the migration it will be added into ToDo property in the Specification window of the Condition element.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p>
````

## PAGE 00607: MODAF

- page_id: `136721097`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721097/MODAF
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:38:03.491+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

According to modeling needs, there are the following MODAF templates for different purposes:

- **MODAF Project**- creating a MODAF project will switch the application to the MODAF graphical user interface and use the MODAF model templates.
- **MODAF Project Template with Process Guide**- using this template, you will get a new MODAF project enhanced with a modeling guide. The modeling guide helps you to build the architecture by providing summary information about the MODAF structure and the architecture creation process.

All MODAF views are described in the following sections:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The modeling guide helps you to build the architecture by providing summary information about the MODAF structure and the architecture creation process.</span></li></ul><p>All MODAF views are described in the following sections:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="f0651189-a11f-4a3d-8db1-b31992b004d1" /></p><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721098 space=UAF12P2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00609: MODAF All Views Viewpoint

- page_id: `136721098`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721098/MODAF+All+Views+Viewpoint
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:16:57.045+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

411478012

411478014

411478013

Elements that are part of the All View. The All-Views (AVs) provide an overarching description of the architecture, its scope, ownership, timeframe and all of the other meta data that is required in order to effectively search and query architectural models. They also provide a place to record any findings arising from the architecting process. The AVs include a dictionary of the terms used in the construction of the architecture – which helps others fully understand its meaning at a later date. Since the AVs provide critical information for the future access and exploitation of an architectural model their population is essential whenever an architecture is created or modified. The AVs provide a critical input into the processes that provide architectural governance.

411478011

**Views**

****

The All-Views (AVs) provide an overarching description of the architecture, its scope, ownership, timeframe and all of the other meta data that is required in order to effectively search and query architectural models. They also provide a place to record any findings arising from the architecting process. The AVs include a dictionary of the terms used in the construction of the architecture – which helps others fully understand its meaning at a later date. Since the AVs provide critical information for the future access and exploitation of an architectural model their population is essential whenever an architecture is created or modified. The AVs provide a critical input into the processes that provide architectural governance.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f02a8fdd-c112-436c-a4fb-641f76417ef7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">411478011</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Views</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b73aa97c-98b2-4c26-8583-8edf93fd9486" /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721194 space=UAF12P2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00610: MODAF Operational Viewpoint

- page_id: `136721194`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721194/MODAF+Operational+Viewpoint
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:17:36.161+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413846051

413846053

413846052

The Operational Viewpoint is about real-world activities, the people and machinery that perform them, and the means by which they are performed. The Operational Viewpoint is divided into nine views intended to answer the “who”, “what”, “when”, “where”, “why”, and “how” of a mission.

The Operational Views are common to MODAF and DoDAF; OV-1b, OV-1c, and OV-2 however, have been customized to provide for MOD requirements. The Operational Views describe the tasks and activities, operational elements, and information exchanges required to conduct operations. In MODAF thinking, the OV Views are considered to illustrate the Logical Architecture of the enterprise.

413846050

**View**

****

In MODAF thinking, the OV Views are considered to illustrate the Logical Architecture of the enterprise.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="51021151-7b24-4f28-ab31-f3d730d0b90f"><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jrpPuZ8Vf74" /></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e6cdcbef-4985-4c8b-8b96-f77d4cc309bf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413846050</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>View</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="3b863d1e-d17a-438e-8824-16c3bb548b69" /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

417346697

The Services View (SOV) shows the elements that are part of SOV-1 through SOV-5.

The Service-Orientated View is a description of services needed to directly support the operational domain as described in the Operational View. A service is described as a unit of work through which a particular Resource provides a useful result to a consuming Resource.

- labels: []

411757999

411758001

411758000

The Strategic View (StV) shows the elements that are part of StV-1 through StV-6. The Strategic Elements are used in the Strategic View that provides an overall Enterprise Architecture assessment of the Capabilities and their relationships facilitating Capability Management (e.g. capability introduction, integration, re-alignment and removal). While an Enterprise will have a number of UAF Architecture Descriptions that have the Operational, System, Technical Standards, and All Views, only one Strategic View will exist across a number of Architecture Descriptions.

411757997

**Views**

419208934

419208936

419208935

The System View (SV) shows the elements that are part of SV-1 through SV-12. Models in the System Viewpoint represent alternate realizations in terms of equipment capability of the operational capabilities expressed through models in the Operational Viewpoint and in the User Requirements. The System Viewpoint primarily addresses the specification of the system capability needed (rather than implementation details). Significant changes originally made in MODAF improved the ability for modelers to represent configuration of capability that include people as well as systems and platforms.

419208933

**Views**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721316 space=UAF12P2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00614: MODAF Technical Standards Viewpoint

- page_id: `136721316`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721316/MODAF+Technical+Standards+Viewpoint
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:40:17.776+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

418729522

418729524

418729523

The Technical View (TV) shows the elements that are part of TV-1 through TV-2.

The Technical View is a set of views delineating standards, rules, notations, and conventions that apply to the implementation of the system architecture. When the standards profile is tied to the system elements to which they apply, TV-1 serves as the bridge between the SV and TV. SV-9 forecasts relate to the TV-1 in that a timed technology forecast may contribute to the decision to retire or phase out the use of a certain standard in connection with a system element. Similarly, SV-9 forecasts relate to TV-2 standards forecasts in that a certain standard may be adopted depending on a certain technology becoming available (e.g., the availability of Java Script may influence the decision to adopt a new HTML standard).

MODAF extends the core DoDAF Technical Standards Views to include non-technical standards and policies applicable to the architecture such as operational doctrine, industry process standards, etc. Additionally, the TV-1 may also document policies and standards applicable to the operational or business context. MODAF also distinguishes between ‘applicability’ and ‘conformance’ with regard to architectural elements. If a standard is applicable to a given architecture, that architecture need not be fully conformant with the standard. The degree of conformance to a given standard may be judged on a risk basis at an approval point. An association between a Standard and an architectural element is not to be interpreted as stating the level of compliance of the element is fully compliant with that Standard. Additional evidences would need to be given (outside MODAF) to confirm the level of compliance. Finally, MODAF adds the explicit requirement that any Standards cited in TV-1 View must, where appropriate, be in accordance with the trend towards open architectures – i.e. standards which encourage stove-piped systems are expressly prohibited.

418729521

**Views**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

When the standards profile is tied to the system elements to which they apply, TV-1 serves as the bridge between the SV and TV. SV-9 forecasts relate to the TV-1 in that a timed technology forecast may contribute to the decision to retire or phase out the use of a certain standard in connection with a system element. Similarly, SV-9 forecasts relate to TV-2 standards forecasts in that a certain standard may be adopted depending on a certain technology becoming available (e.g., the availability of Java Script may influence the decision to adopt a new HTML standard).</p><p>MODAF extends the core DoDAF Technical Standards Views to include non-technical standards and policies applicable to the architecture such as operational doctrine, industry process standards, etc. Additionally, the TV-1 may also document policies and standards applicable to the operational or business context. MODAF also distinguishes between ‘applicability’ and ‘conformance’ with regard to architectural elements. If a standard is applicable to a given architecture, that architecture need not be fully conformant with the standard. The degree of conformance to a given standard may be judged on a risk basis at an approval point. An association between a Standard and an architectural element is not to be interpreted as stating the level of compliance of the element is fully compliant with that Standard. Additional evidences would need to be given (outside MODAF) to confirm the level of compliance. Finally, MODAF adds the explicit requirement that any Standards cited in TV-1 View must, where appropriate, be in accordance with the trend towards open architectures – i.e. standards which encourage stove-piped systems are expressly prohibited.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e19db55d-d5b4-4b99-9e7d-ddeed8c8a7d7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">418729521</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Views</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="f0651189-a11f-4a3d-8db1-b31992b004d1" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721180 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00615: MODAF. AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters

- page_id: `136721180`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721180/MODAF.+AcV-1+Acquisition+Clusters
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:53.496+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Acquisition Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413387346

413387349

413387348

**Description**

The Acquisition Clusters (AcV-1) View describes how acquisition projects are grouped in organisational terms as a coherent portfolio of acquisition programmes.

The AcV-1 View provides a way of describing the organizational relationships between multiple acquisition projects, each of which is responsible for delivering individual systems or capabilities. By definition, this View covers acquisition programmes consisting of multiple projects and will generally not be developed by those building Architectures for an individual project. In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.

The AcV-1 View is hierarchical in nature. Higher level groupings of projects (or, rather the organisations that own these projects) form acquisition clusters.

The intent of an AcV-1 View Product is to show:

- All of the acquisition projects delivering systems or system of systems (SoS) within the acquisition programmes under consideration.
- Other systems and SoS which may have a bearing on the Architecture.
- How the systems will be best integrated into acquisition clusters.
- The nesting of acquisition clusters to form a hierarchy.

**Implementation**

AcV-1 can be represented using:

- AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix which is an editable Dependency Matrix.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix

**Related views**

In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.

413387345

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="afd1d3af-f4dc-467b-8fb2-9e2ca67cd031"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413387346</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c54ed0d1-ca28-4a36-91a5-eb7ab785a1b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413387349</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8d3f4a99-74ed-4aa1-930f-994dcdf73953"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413387348</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Acquisition Clusters (AcV-1) View describes how acquisition projects are grouped in organisational terms as a coherent portfolio of acquisition programmes.</p><p>The AcV-1 View provides a way of describing the organizational relationships between multiple acquisition projects, each of which is responsible for delivering individual systems or capabilities. By definition, this View covers acquisition programmes consisting of multiple projects and will generally not be developed by those building Architectures for an individual project. In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.</p><p>The AcV-1 View is hierarchical in nature. Higher level groupings of projects (or, rather the organisations that own these projects) form acquisition clusters.</p><p>The intent of an AcV-1 View Product is to show:</p><ul><li>All of the acquisition projects delivering systems or system of systems (SoS) within the acquisition programmes under consideration.</li><li>Other systems and SoS which may have a bearing on the Architecture.</li><li>How the systems will be best integrated into acquisition clusters.</li><li>The nesting of acquisition clusters to form a hierarchy.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>AcV-1 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix which is an editable Dependency Matrix.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Acv_1.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="44dedf88-c1f1-4a3b-8275-806a92a0fde4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413387345</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Organization" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Post" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Project" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Responsible For" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Project Sequence" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Project" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Project Milestone" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Project Milestone Role" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Project Milestone Role" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Status Indicators" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Creating AcV-1 diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Building AcV-1 matrix" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00616: MODAF. AcV-2 Programme Timelines

- page_id: `136721190`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`

- version_number: 1

- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Acquisition Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413597688

413597690

413597689

**Description**

The AcV-2 view provides a timeline perspective on programmes.

The AcV-2 View is intended primarily to support the acquisition and fielding processes including the management of dependencies between projects and the integration of all the DLODs to achieve a successfully integrated military capability.

For capability-based procurement, these work streams might conveniently be equated with Defence Lines of Development (DLODs).

**Implementation**

AcV-2 can be represented using an AcV-2 diagram which is realized as a Gantt Chart.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Acv-2 Programme Timelines

**Related views**

The use of AcV-2 should support the management of capability delivery and be aligned with the capability phasing view (StV-3), if one exists.

413597687

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721108 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00617: MODAF. AV-1 Overview and Summary Information

- page_id: `136721108`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721108/MODAF.+AV-1+Overview+and+Summary+Information
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:50.675+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF All Views Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

411544091

411544094

411544092

**Description**

The overview and summary information contained within the AV-1 product provides an executive-level summary information in a consistent form that allows for quick reference and comparison between architectural descriptions. AV-1 includes assumptions, constraints, and limitations that may affect high-level decisions relating to an architecture. In an enterprise repository environment, individual architectures are mapped against enterprise phases to provide context between the architectures. AV-1 is usually a structured text product. Organizations may create a template for the AV-1 that can then be used to create a consistent set of information across different architecture-based projects.

**Implementation**

AV-1 can be represented:

- Using a report, which is automatically generated from all data. Since only a partial AV-1 report can be generated, the rest of data must be filled in manually.
- Using a AV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of AV-1 Overview and Summary Information report

411544089

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Architectural Description' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Architecture Metadata' space='UAF12P2024x']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing reports' space='UAF12P2024x']

## PAGE 00618: MODAF. AV-2 Integrated Dictionary

- page_id: `136721110`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721110/MODAF.+AV-2+Integrated+Dictionary
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:50.852+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF All Views Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

411641246

411641249

411641248

**Description**

An AV-2 presents all the Elements used in an architecture as a standalone structure. An AV-2 presents all the Elements as a specialization hierarchy, provides a text definition for each one and references the source of the element. An AV-2 shows elements from the Ontology that have been used in the architecture and new elements that have been introduced by the architecture. Architectures often introduce new terms – usually because the architecture is covering new technology or business processes. The purpose of the AV-2 is to provide a local extension of the Ontology to explain the terms and abbreviations used in building the architecture.

**Implementation**

AV-2 can be represented:

- Using a report, which is automatically generated from all data.
- An AV-2 table. If you have AV-2 diagrams from previous versions, they will be migrated into the AV-1 diagram when loading the project with UPDM 17.0.2.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### AV-2 Integrated Dictionary

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of AV-2 Integrated Dictionary report

## PAGE 00619: MODAF. OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic

- page_id: `136721195`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721195/MODAF.+OV-1+High-Level+Operational+Concept+Graphic
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.162+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413963572

413963574

413963573

**Description**

The purpose of High-level Operational Concept Graphic is to provide a high-level graphical and textual description of operational concept (high level organizations, missions, geographic configuration, connectivity, etc) of what the architecture is supposed to do, and how it is supposed to do it. The OV-1, along with the corresponding AV-1 product is intended to serve as an executive summary of the architecture.

**Implementation**

OV-1 can be represented using:

- Link to an external document.
- OV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- OV-1 Free Form diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- UML Composite Structure Diagram.

**Sample** 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic

413963571

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='High Level Operational Concept' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Role' space='UAF12P2024x']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating OV-1 diagram' space='UAF12P2024x']

The OV-1, along with the corresponding AV-1 product is intended to serve as an executive summary of the architecture.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-1 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>Link to an external document.</li><li>OV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>OV-1 Free Form diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>UML Composite Structure Diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="OV_1.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721199 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00620: MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description

- page_id: `136721199`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721199/MODAF.+OV-2+Operational+Node+Internal+Relationship+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.235+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414117534

414117536

414117535

**Implementation**

OV-2 can be represented using:

- OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- UML Composite Structure diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.
- SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description

**Related views**

An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.

Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.

The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.

The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.

414117533

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721201 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00621: MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description

- page_id: `136721201`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721201/MODAF.+OV-2+Operational+Node+Relationship+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.287+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414271992

414271994

414271993

**Description**

The Operational Node Relationship Description tracks the need to exchange information from specific operational nodes (that play a key role in the architecture) to others. An OV-2 does not depict the connectivity between the nodes. MODAF modifies the OV-2 in two ways. First, it recommends that an OV-2 diagram (now OV2a) shows the platforms or geographic locations at which operational nodes are deployed. Secondly, it provides additional information (OV-2b) about each needline in the form of a requirements specification. There are now four types of needlines identified as follows:

1. InformationExchange
2. EnergyFlow
3. MaterielFlow
4. MovementOfPeople

In addition, MODAF permits service-oriented architectures. Instead of needlines between nodes, it is possible to show which services the nodes provide and consume. Finally, MODAF again permits known resources to be shown in an OV-2. However, this must be clearly shown as a KnownResource in an OV-2 model. LogicalArchitecture, which is the container class for all the nodes and KnownResources, is introduced.

**Implementation**

OV-2 can be represented using:

- OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- UML Composite Structure diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.
- SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description

**Related views**

An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swimlanes on the activities.

Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.

The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.

The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.

414271991

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Architecture' space='UAF12P2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721205 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00622: MODAF. OV-3 Operational Information Exchange Matrix

- page_id: `136721205`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721205/MODAF.+OV-3+Operational+Information+Exchange+Matrix
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.372+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414509731

414509733

414509732

**Description**

Information exchanges express the relationship across the three basic architecture data elements of an OV (operational activities, operational nodes, and information flow) with a focus on the specific aspects of the information flow and the information content.

The Information Exchanges of the OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes shown in the OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes).

**Implementation**

OV-3 can be represented using:

- OV-3 table.
- OV-3 role-based table. A role-based table represents exchanges between Node Roles (node usages).
- OV-3 spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-3 Operational Information Exchange Matrix

**Related views**

An OV-3 is initially constructed from the information contained in the Operational Node Connectivity Description (OV-2).

414509730

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='UAF12P2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721208 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00623: MODAF. OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart

- page_id: `136721208`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721208/MODAF.+OV-4+Organizational+Relationships+Chart
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.448+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414660137

414660139

414660138

**Description**

The Organizational Relationships Chart illustrates the command structure or relationships (as opposed to relationships with respect to a business process flow) among human roles, organizations, or organization types that are the key players in architecture. MODAF divides The OV-4 in two views: an OV-4 Typical and an OV-4 Actual. The former is exactly as the DoDAF OV-4, while the latter is a special form of the SV-1; where the resources are restricted to being organizational.

**Implementation**

OV-4 can be represented using:

- OV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart

**Related views**

The organizations that are modeled using an OV-4 in the Operational Viewpoint may also appear in other views, for example, SV-1 (organizational constituents of a capability configuration), AcV-1 (actual organizations that own projects), and SV-5 (organizational resources performs functions).

414660136

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Organization' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Post' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Person' space='UAF12P2024x']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actual Resource Relationship' space='UAF12P2024x']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Activity' space='UAF12P2024x']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instantiating structures' space='UAF12P2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="29b4798f-4968-473e-942b-5046ca519623"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414660137</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="98613f56-bf0c-48fd-832c-1115c990183f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414660139</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72e5a615-a343-4a34-a6af-dd9426db78e5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414660138</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Organizational Relationships Chart illustrates the command structure or relationships (as opposed to relationships with respect to a business process flow) among human roles, organizations, or organization types that are the key players in architecture. MODAF divides The OV-4 in two views: an OV-4 Typical and an OV-4 Actual. The former is exactly as the DoDAF OV-4, while the latter is a special form of the SV-1; where the resources are restricted to being organizational.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-4 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>OV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>UML Class diagram.</li><li>SysML Block Definition diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_4.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The organizations that are modeled using an OV-4 in the Operational Viewpoint may also appear in other views, for example, SV-1 (organizational constituents of a capability configuration), AcV-1 (actual organizations that own projects), and SV-5 (organizational resources performs functions).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ac4d9e94-934e-4eaa-beb3-840ee0ccf2c5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414660136</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Organization" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Post" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Person" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Command" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Competence" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Provides Competence" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Requires Competence" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Organization" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Post" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Person" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Fills Post" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Resource Relationship" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Actual Organization Role" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Owns Process" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Working with OV-4 diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721211 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00624: MODAF. OV-5 Operational Activity Model

- page_id: `136721211`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721211/MODAF.+OV-5+Operational+Activity+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.526+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414777526

414777528

414777527

**Description**

The Operational Activity Model describes the operations that are normally conducted in the course of achieving a mission or a business goal, from a net-centric perspective. It describes capabilities, operational activities (or tasks), input and output (I/O) flows between activities, and I/O flows to/from activities that are outside the scope of the architecture. It is imperative that the levels-of-detail between the OV-2, OV-3, and OV-5 remain cohesive. For example, if one diagram of OV-2 operational nodes is developed that shows aggregated organizations only, then it is imperative that the corresponding OV-5 product be developed to show only those operational activities that are meaningful with respect to these operational nodes. Similarly, the information exchanges of OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes depicted in OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes). The net-centric OV-5 may be used in the following ways:

- Delineate lines of dependency on external activities when coupled with an OV-2.
- Highlight information flows to depict the status of the information's refinement (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Provide the critical foundation for depicting Task, Post, Process, and Use (TPPU) activity sequencing and timing in the OV-6a, OV-6b, and OV-6c.
- Identify critical mission threads and operational information exchanges by annotating which activities are critical, i.e., identify the activities in the model that are critical.

**Implementation**

OV-5 can be represented using:

- OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity hierarchies. This diagram is based on the UML Class diagram.
- OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- UML Activity diagram.
- SysML Block diagram.
- SysML Activity diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-5 Operational Activity hierarchy Model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of OV-5 Operational Activity Model

**Related views**

The OV-5 and OV-2 are, to a degree, complements of each other. An OV-5 focuses on the operational activities whereas OV-2 focuses on the operational nodes. Due to the relationship between nodes and operational activities, these types of view should normally be developed together.

To maintain this independence from implementation, the logical Nodes in an OV-2 are used to represent the structure which carries out the Operational Activities. Operational Activities are realized as Functions (SV-4) which are the “how” to the Operational Activities’ “what”, for example, they are specified in terms of the resources that carry them out.

The activities described in an OV-5 may be Standard Operational Activities which are defined in the StV-6 (which also maps the activities to corresponding capabilities).

414777524

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721229 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00625: MODAF. OV-6a Operational Rules Model

- page_id: `136721229`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721229/MODAF.+OV-6a+Operational+Rules+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.742+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414963391

414963393

414963392

**Description**

The Operational Rules Model specifies operational or business rules that are constraints on an enterprise, a mission, operation, business, or an architecture. While other OV views (e.g., OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5) describe the structure of a business—what the business can do—for the most part, they do not describe what the business must do, or what it cannot do. At the mission level, an OV-6a may consist of doctrine, guidance, rules of engagement, and so forth. At the operation level, rules may include such things as a military Operational Plan (OPLAN). At lower levels, an OV-6a describes the rules under which the architecture or its nodes behave under specified conditions. Such rules can be expressed in a textual form, for example, “If (these conditions) exist, and (this event) occurs, then (perform these actions).” At a top level, rules should at least embody the concepts of operations defined in an OV-1, and should provide guidelines for the development and definition of more detailed 
rules and behavioral definitions that will occur later in the architecture definition process.

**Implementation**

OV-6a can be represented using:

- OV-6a table.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='OV-6a Operational Node Parametric' space='UAF12P2024x'] .
- OV-6a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6a Operational Rules Model

**Related views**

An OV-6a constrains the structure elements of OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5. OV-6a can also be used to extend the capture of business requirements by constraining the structure and validity of the OV-7 elements.

As the View name implies, the rules captured in an OV-6a are operational (i.e., mission-oriented) whereas resource-oriented rules are defined in an SV-10 (OV-6 is the “what” to SV-10’s “how”).

**Constraint owner**

You can choose where all the constraints will be stored through the**Project Options**.

To select a constraint owner

1. In the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Go to General > UAF , and in the Default Constraint Owners property group, choose a needed owner.

414963390

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Constraint' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Activity' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Information' space='UAF12P2024x']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with OV-6a table' space='UAF12P2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721233 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00626: MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

- page_id: `136721233`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721233/MODAF.+OV-6b+Operational+State+Transition+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.934+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

415053735

415053738

415053736

**Description**

The Operational State Transition Description is a graphical method of describing how an operational node or activity responds to various events by changing its state. The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.

Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.

**Implementation**

OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

**Related views**

An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).

415053734

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational State Description' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational States' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='UAF12P2024x']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors' space='UAF12P2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="79d1d966-25d0-4fb2-bac4-5de0d10b410f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">415053735</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b8fec49-0a56-4712-aee0-75a27a7fdb3f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">415053738</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b2fe82e0-fc74-43b3-988f-3b191014ec24"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">415053736</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Operational State Transition Description is a graphical method of describing how an operational node or activity responds to various events by changing its state. The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.</p><p>Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6b.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6b Operational State Transition Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="57f8294b-50b4-48ab-bdb2-8b31cfff3677"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">415053734</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational State Description" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational States" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational Performer" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721235 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00627: MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

- page_id: `136721235`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721235/MODAF.+OV-6c+Operational+Event-Trace+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:54.998+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

415390576

415390578

415390577

**Description**

The Operational Event-Trace Description provides a time-ordered examination of the information exchanges between participating operational nodes as a result of a particular operational thread or scenario. Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.

The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following:

- Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.
- Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.
- Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.

**Implementation**

OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

**Related views**

The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.

The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.

415390575

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space='CST2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Message' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Role' space='UAF12P2024x']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Realized Operational Exchange wizard' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange Manager dialog' space='UAF12P2024x']

Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.</p><p>The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following: </p><ul><li>Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.</li><li>Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).</li><li>Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.</li><li>Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6c.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.</p><p>The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="236a8efa-bc13-4714-96b6-6063289a3aed"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">415390575</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2024x" ri:content-title="Interaction" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational Message" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational Exchange" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational Performer" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational Role" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related references</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Realized Operational Exchange wizard" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational Exchange Manager dialog" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721237 space=UAF12P2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00628: MODAF. OV-7 Information Model

- page_id: `136721237`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721237/MODAF.+OV-7+Information+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:55.070+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

416310414

416310417

416310415

**Description**

The Logical Data Model describes the structure of an architecture domain’s system data types and the structural business process rules (defined in the architecture’s Operational View) that govern the system data. It provides a definition of architecture domain data types, their attributes or characteristics, and their interrelationships. An OV7, including the domain’s system data types or entity definitions, is a key element in supporting interoperability between architectures, since these definitions may be used by other organizations to determine system data compatibility. Often, different organizations may use the same entity name to mean very different kinds of system data with different internal structure. This situation will pose significant interoperability risks, as the system data 
models may appear to be compatible, each having a Target Track data entity but having different and incompatible interpretations of what Target Track means.

In the NCE, the OV-7 describes the structure of data types (information elements) for information being made available or being consumed by the OV-5 activities and provides the organization and composition of metadata that can be used to characterize the information exchanged in the NCE.

****

**Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration**

You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in OV-7.

Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in OV-7. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.

**Implementation**

OV-7 can be represented using:

- OV-7 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### OV-7 Information Model

**Related views**

An Operational Information Entity within an OV-7 may be an Information Element in an OV-3 or an Activity Flow Object in an OV-5.

Note that MODAF talks about ‘information’ in the Operational Viewpoint and ‘data’ in the System Viewpoint. The intention of this is that OV-7 describes information or data of importance to the business (e.g., information products that might be referred to in doctrine, SOPs, etc.) whereas SV-11 describes data relevant at the system level.

416310413

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Model' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Measurement' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Information' space='UAF12P2024x']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with OV-7 diagram' space='UAF12P2024x']

This situation will pose significant interoperability risks, as the system data<br />models may appear to be compatible, each having a Target Track data entity but having different and incompatible interpretations of what Target Track means.</p><p>In the NCE, the OV-7 describes the structure of data types (information elements) for information being made available or being consumed by the OV-5 activities and provides the organization and composition of metadata that can be used to characterize the information exchanged in the NCE.</p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration</strong></p><p>You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in OV-7.</p><p>Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in OV-7. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-7 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>OV-7 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>UML Class diagram.</li><li>SysML Block Definition diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_7.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-7 Information Model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></strong></p><h6>OV-7 Information Model</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An Operational Information Entity within an OV-7 may be an Information Element in an OV-3 or an Activity Flow Object in an OV-5.</p><p>Note that MODAF talks about ‘information’ in the Operational Viewpoint and ‘data’ in the System Viewpoint. The intention of this is that OV-7 describes information or data of importance to the business (e.g., information products that might be referred to in doctrine, SOPs, etc.) whereas SV-11 describes data relevant at the system level.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="206424e6-9c70-4f67-a0b7-e4517a41ecd4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">416310413</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Information Model" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Measurement" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Association" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Operational Information" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Working with OV-7 diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00629: MODAF. SOV-1 Service Taxonomy

- page_id: `136721242`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721242/MODAF.+SOV-1+Service+Taxonomy
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:42:40.057+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

417549592

417549594

417549593

**Description**

The Service Taxonomy View (SOV-1) specifies a hierarchy of services. The elements in the hierarchy are service specifications (i.e. service interfaces), and the relationships between the elements are specializations – i.e. one Service is a special type of another. Along with SOV-2, it specifies a standard library of Service specifications for an enterprise, which Service implementers are expected to conform to.

**Implementation**

SOV-1 can be represented using a SOV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-1 Service Taxonomy

**Related views**

The Services Interfaces in SOV-1 can realize Capabilities (from StV-2) and also support the Operational Activities (from OV-5).

417549591

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Capability' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Expose' space='SYSMLP2024x']

**Related procedures**

SOV-1 Service Taxonomy" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-1 Service Taxonomy</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The Services Interfaces in SOV-1 can realize Capabilities (from StV-2) and also support the Operational Activities (from OV-5).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bf20ffc6-d95d-4cb4-aa14-11cd13314b28"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">417549591</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Service Interface" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Capability" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024x" ri:content-title="Expose" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="f0651189-a11f-4a3d-8db1-b31992b004d1" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00630: MODAF. SOV-2 Service Interface Specification

- page_id: `136721245`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721245/MODAF.+SOV-2+Service+Interface+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-21T10:28:55.393+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

417613614

417613617

417613616

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Interface Specification View (SOV-2) is to define the interfaces presented by a service. A Service presents one or more interfaces to consumers (a “consumer” being any agent capable of using the service – a person, an organization, a system or another service). In this case, the architect specifies the provided interfaces. A service may also be capable of using interfaces exposed by other services, and the architect may specify these as used interfaces.

**Implementation**

SOV-2 can be represented using

- Report which is automatically generated from all data
- SOV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-2 Service Interface Specification

417613613

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Method' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Parameter' space='UAF12P2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Port' space='UAF12P2024x']

In this case, the architect specifies the provided interfaces. A service may also be capable of using interfaces exposed by other services, and the architect may specify these as used interfaces.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-2 can be represented using</p><ul><li>Report which is automatically generated from all data</li><li>SOV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_2.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-2 Service Interface Specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-2 Service Interface Specification</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9e9ef6e0-0653-4082-a361-9f0f2c188a3b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">417613613</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Service Interface" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Service Method" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Service Parameter" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="Resource Port" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721247 space=UAF12P2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00631: MODAF. SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping

- page_id: `136721247`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721247/MODAF.+SOV-3+Capability+to+Service+Mapping
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:42:22.579+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

417931562

417931564

417931563

**Description**

The Capability to Service Mapping View (SOV-3) depicts which services contribute to the achievement of a capability. It is in the form of a table generated from the database. If a network-enabled capability is to be delivered by the orchestration of loosely coupled services (i.e. a service-oriented architecture), it is essential to know which services have the potential to support particular capabilities. This helps to prevent redundant services or capabilities, (except where specifically required) and what is known as stovepipe development. An SOV-3 presents a simple mapping of services to capabilities, showing which services contribute to which capability.

**Implementation**

SOV-3 can be represented using:

- The SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements, and the Capabilities will be used as the column elements.
- The SOV-3 Services to Service Contracts Mapping matrix. The Services to Service Contracts Mapping matrix depicts mapping between the Services or their specializations and the Service Contracts they govern.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping

417931561

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Expose' space='SYSMLP2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Capability' space='']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Implied Relations' space='MD2024x']

It is in the form of a table generated from the database. If a network-enabled capability is to be delivered by the orchestration of loosely coupled services (i.e. a service-oriented architecture), it is essential to know which services have the potential to support particular capabilities. This helps to prevent redundant services or capabilities, (except where specifically required) and what is known as stovepipe development. An SOV-3 presents a simple mapping of services to capabilities, showing which services contribute to which capability.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-3 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>The SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements, and the Capabilities will be used as the column elements.</li><li>The SOV-3 Services to Service Contracts Mapping matrix. <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The<span> </span></span>Services to Service Contracts Mapping<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> matrix depicts mapping between the Services or their specializations and the Service Contracts they govern.</span></li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_3.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="UAF12P2024x" ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="200e6de5-4602-495b-a1f4-42b1a3b1ac8f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">417931561</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Service Interface" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024x" ri:content-title="Expose" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Capability" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="f0651189-a11f-4a3d-8db1-b31992b004d1" /></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Using Implied Relations" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136721252 space=UAF12P2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00632: MODAF. SOV-4a Service Constraints

- page_id: `136721252`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024x/pages/136721252/MODAF.+SOV-4a+Service+Constraints
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:32:52.369+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

418190171

418190174

418190172

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Constraints View (SOV-4a) is to specify constraints that apply to providers and consumers of services. To better enable consistency and re-use of service specifications, it is important to set constraints on how a service should behave. An SOV-4a product specifies constraints against services to which implementations of the service must conform.

**Implementation**

SOV-4a can be represented using:

- SOV-4a table.
- SOV-4a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4a Service Constraints

**Constraint owner**

You can choose where all the constraints will be stored through the**Project Options**.

To select a constraint owner

1. In the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Go to General > UAF , and in the Default Constraint Owners property group, choose a needed owner.

418190170

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Policy' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='']

**Related procedures**
